content.text: What an Availability Set Is

An Availability Set is a logical grouping of VMs that tells Azure to distribute them across separate fault domains and update domains within a single datacenter. If you deploy two or more VMs in the same Availability Set, Azure guarantees that not all of them will be affected by a single hardware failure (fault domain) or by a single round of planned maintenance (update domain).

Availability Sets protect against two distinct failure modes: unplanned hardware failures (a rack power strip fails, taking down all servers plugged into it) and planned platform updates (Azure rebooting hosts to apply firmware or hypervisor patches). Fault domains address the former; update domains address the latter.

attrs.alt: Availability Set with 3 Fault Domains and 3 Update Domains: 9 VMs placed so no single FD or UD contains all VMs

content.text: Fault Domains

A fault domain is a group of servers that share the same power source and network switch — typically one physical rack. If the power distribution unit (PDU) for that rack fails, or the top-of-rack switch fails, every server in that rack goes offline simultaneously. This is the blast radius of a fault domain failure.

Azure spreads VMs across fault domains automatically when they are placed in an Availability Set. With 2 VMs in a 2-fault-domain set (the default minimum), each VM is in a different rack. A rack-level power failure takes down 1 of 2 VMs; the other continues serving traffic.
Default fault domains:
2 per Availability Set. You can configure up to 3.
Platform guarantee:
Azure will not place two VMs from the same Availability Set in the same fault domain if enough fault domains are available.
Physical scope:
fault domains protect against rack-level failures (power, switch). They do not protect against datacenter-level or zone-level failures.
Managed disks:

when you use managed disks with an Availability Set, Azure also distributes the underlying storage stamp across separate fault domains, so a storage fault domain failure does not take down both the VM and its disk.
Update Domains

An update domain is a logical group of VMs that Azure will reboot together during planned maintenance. Azure must occasionally update host firmware, apply microcode patches, or refresh the hypervisor. It does this by rebooting one update domain at a time, waiting for that domain to come back online before proceeding to the next.

With 5 update domains (the default) and 5 VMs in an Availability Set, only 1 VM is rebooted at any given time during a platform update cycle. The remaining 4 VMs continue serving traffic throughout the maintenance window.
Default update domains:
5 per Availability Set. You can configure up to 20.
Sequential rollout:
Azure reboots one UD, confirms health, then proceeds to the next. This can make a full maintenance cycle take hours to days across all UDs.
Zero control over timing:
you cannot choose when a platform update runs, but Service Health provides 35-day advance notice for events that require reboots.
SLA from update domains:
because Azure never reboots all VMs simultaneously, the 99.95% SLA for Availability Sets is partly underwritten by the UD guarantee of sequential updates.
Creating and Using an Availability Set

# Create an Availability Set with 3 fault domains and 5 update domains
az vm availability-set create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAvailabilitySet \
  --location eastus \
  --platform-fault-domain-count 3 \
  --platform-update-domain-count 5

# Deploy the first VM into the Availability Set
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1 \
  --availability-set myAvailabilitySet \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Deploy a second VM (will be placed in a different fault domain)
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-2 \
  --availability-set myAvailabilitySet \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Verify the fault domain and update domain assignments
az vm availability-set show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAvailabilitySet \
  --query "{faultDomains:platformFaultDomainCount, updateDomains:platformUpdateDomainCount, vms:virtualMachines[*].id}"

# Check individual VM placement
az vm show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1 \
  --query "instanceView.platformFaultDomain"

az vm show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1 \
  --query "instanceView.platformUpdateDomain"
Availability Sets vs Availability Zones

Availability Sets and Availability Zones both provide VM redundancy, but they protect at different scopes and involve different trade-offs. Choosing between them depends on whether your region has Availability Zones and what failure scenarios you need to survive.
Dimension
Availability Set
Availability Zones
Failure scope protected
Rack failure (power strip, ToR switch)
Entire datacenter building failure
Physical scope
Single datacenter; VMs in the same building
Separate physical buildings kilometers apart
Region requirement
Any region with VMs
Only regions with 3 Availability Zones
SLA
99.95% for 2+ VMs
99.99% for 2+ VMs across zones
Network latency between VMs
Sub-millisecond (same building)
1-2ms (different buildings)
Cross-VM data transfer cost
None
$0.01/GB between zones
Update domain control
Yes (1 UD at a time during updates)
No (platform handles per-zone updates independently)
Max VMs per group
No stated limit
No stated limit
Use case
Regions without AZs; latency-sensitive co-location
Regions with AZs; zone-level failure protection

If your region has Availability Zones, use zones instead of Availability Sets for new workloads. Zones offer a higher SLA (99.99% vs 99.95%) and protect against broader failure scenarios. Availability Sets are the right choice in regions that predate AZ support, or when sub-millisecond VM-to-VM latency is required.
Managed Disk Integration

When you use managed disks with VMs in an Availability Set, Azure automatically aligns disk fault domains with VM fault domains. This ensures that a storage fault domain failure — which would take down the underlying storage cluster for that disk — does not also be in the same fault domain as the VM using that disk.

With unmanaged disks (legacy), this alignment does not happen. Both the VM and disk could end up in the same fault domain, meaning a single rack failure takes out both, which makes the Availability Set protection ineffective. Always use managed disks with Availability Sets.

# Verify that VMs in an Availability Set use managed disks
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[?availabilitySet!=null].{name:name, managedDisks:storageProfile.osDisk.managedDisk}" \
  --output table

# Create a VM with explicit managed disk (default for modern CLI)
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-managed \
  --availability-set myAvailabilitySet \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --os-disk-size-gb 64 \
  --storage-sku Premium_LRS \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys
Load Balancing with Availability Sets

An Availability Set alone does not distribute traffic across VMs. You must pair it with an Azure Load Balancer to direct incoming requests to healthy VMs. The load balancer's health probes detect when a VM goes offline and remove it from the backend pool, while continuing to send traffic to the remaining VMs.

# Create a Standard Load Balancer for VMs in an Availability Set
# 1. Create public IP
az network public-ip create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLB-PIP \
  --sku Standard \
  --allocation-method Static

# 2. Create the load balancer
az network lb create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLB \
  --sku Standard \
  --public-ip-address myLB-PIP \
  --frontend-ip-name myFrontend \
  --backend-pool-name myBackendPool

# 3. Add a health probe (HTTP on port 80)
az network lb probe create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --name myHealthProbe \
  --protocol Http \
  --port 80 \
  --path /health

# 4. Add a load balancing rule
az network lb rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --name myHTTPRule \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --frontend-port 80 \
  --backend-port 80 \
  --frontend-ip-name myFrontend \
  --backend-pool-name myBackendPool \
  --probe-name myHealthProbe

# 5. Add VM NICs to the backend pool
az network nic ip-config address-pool add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nic-name myVM-1-NIC \
  --ip-config-name ipconfig1 \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --address-pool myBackendPool
Monitoring Availability Set Health

# Check the health status of all VMs in a resource group (includes AS membership)
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[*].{name:name, powerState:powerState, as:availabilitySet.id}" \
  --show-details \
  --output table

# Get platform fault domain and update domain for each VM
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[?availabilitySet!=null].[name, instanceView.platformFaultDomain, instanceView.platformUpdateDomain]" \
  --show-details \
  --output table 2>/dev/null || \
az vm list -g myRG --query "[*].name" -o tsv | while read VM; do
  FD=$(az vm show -g myRG -n "$VM" --query "instanceView.platformFaultDomain" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  UD=$(az vm show -g myRG -n "$VM" --query "instanceView.platformUpdateDomain" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  echo "$VM  FD=$FD  UD=$UD"
done

# Set up an alert for when any VM in the resource group becomes unavailable
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "VMHealthAlert" \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG" \
  --condition "category=ResourceHealth" \
  --action-groups /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/myAG
IaC: Availability Sets in Bicep and Terraform
Bicep
Terraform
Common Mistakes
Single VM in an Availability Set:
a single VM gets no SLA benefit from the AS. You need at least 2 VMs for the 99.95% SLA to apply.
Using unmanaged disks:
unmanaged disk fault domains are not aligned with VM fault domains. Always use managed disks (sku: Aligned) with Availability Sets.
Mixing different applications in one AS:

an AS groups VMs that serve the same role. Mixing web servers and database VMs in one AS means a fault domain failure takes down one of each, leaving a partial system. Use separate Availability Sets per tier.
Expecting Availability Zones level protection:
AS protects within a single datacenter. A zone-level failure (entire datacenter building down) affects all VMs in an AS. Use Availability Zones for zone-level resilience.
Adding a VM to an AS after creation:
VMs cannot be added to or removed from an Availability Set after creation. The AS must be specified at VM deployment time.
Forgetting the load balancer:
VMs in an AS are not automatically behind a load balancer. Without a load balancer and health probes, traffic will not automatically redirect to healthy VMs after a fault domain failure.
Multi-Tier Architecture with Availability Sets

Production workloads typically span multiple tiers: web/application, middleware, and database. Each tier should have its own Availability Set so that a fault domain failure in the web tier does not also take down a database VM. Mixing tiers in one AS means a rack failure might remove one VM from each tier simultaneously, leaving a partially broken system that is harder to diagnose than a clean failure.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Three-tier Availability Set architecture

RG="myRG"
LOCATION="eastus"
IMAGE="Ubuntu2204"
SIZE="Standard_DS2_v2"

# Create AS per tier
az vm availability-set create -g $RG -n web-as   -l $LOCATION --platform-fault-domain-count 3 --platform-update-domain-count 5
az vm availability-set create -g $RG -n app-as   -l $LOCATION --platform-fault-domain-count 3 --platform-update-domain-count 5
az vm availability-set create -g $RG -n db-as    -l $LOCATION --platform-fault-domain-count 2 --platform-update-domain-count 5

# Web tier: 3 VMs across 3 fault domains
for i in 1 2 3; do
  az vm create -g $RG -n web-vm-$i --availability-set web-as \
    --image $IMAGE --size $SIZE \
    --admin-username azureuser --generate-ssh-keys
done

# Application tier: 3 VMs
for i in 1 2 3; do
  az vm create -g $RG -n app-vm-$i --availability-set app-as \
    --image $IMAGE --size $SIZE \
    --admin-username azureuser --generate-ssh-keys
done

# Database tier: 2 VMs (primary + secondary for replication)
for i in 1 2; do
  az vm create -g $RG -n db-vm-$i --availability-set db-as \
    --image $IMAGE --size Standard_E8s_v3 \
    --admin-username azureuser --generate-ssh-keys
done

echo "Verifying fault domain spread..."
for TIER in web app db; do
  echo "--- $TIER tier ---"
  az vm list -g $RG --query "[?contains(name, '$TIER')].{name:name}" -o tsv | while read VM; do
    FD=$(az vm show -g $RG -n "$VM" --query "instanceView.platformFaultDomain" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
    echo "  $VM: FD=$FD"
  done
done
Availability Sets and Azure Site Recovery

Azure Site Recovery (ASR) can replicate VMs from an Availability Set in one region to a target region for DR purposes. When ASR replicates an AS, it respects the fault domain and update domain structure at the target region, placing replicated VMs in an AS at the recovery site.

# Enable replication for a VM in an AS (using ASR REST API via az rest)
# Pre-requisite: Recovery Services Vault already created in the target region

# Enable replication (simplified; full config requires a detailed JSON body)
az rest \
  --method put \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.RecoveryServices/vaults/myVault/replicationProtectedItems/myVM-1?api-version=2022-10-01" \
  --body @replication-config.json

# List protected items in a vault (shows replication state)
az rest \
  --method get \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.RecoveryServices/vaults/myVault/replicationProtectedItems?api-version=2022-10-01" \
  --query "value[*].{name:name, state:properties.protectionState, health:properties.replicationHealth}" \
  --output table

When planning DR with ASR for Availability Set VMs, configure the target Availability Set at the recovery site before enabling replication. ASR will use the target AS to place recovered VMs across fault domains, preserving the same redundancy posture at the recovery site.
SLA Calculation: What the 99.95% Actually Means

The 99.95% SLA for Availability Sets applies when you have 2 or more VMs deployed in the same AS. This SLA means Microsoft commits to at least one VM being reachable 99.95% of the time per month. The math:
99.95% uptime = 0.05% allowable downtime per month
0.05% of 720 hours (30-day month) = 0.36 hours = about 21.6 minutes per month of allowable total downtime
This is the SLA for the set, not each individual VM
A single VM with no AS has no SLA commitment from Microsoft; a single VM with a Premium SSD has 99.9% SLA

The SLA applies to unplanned hardware failures (fault domain protection) and planned maintenance downtime (update domain protection). It does not cover: outages caused by application bugs, guest OS failures, misconfigured NSGs, or datacenter-level (zone-level) events.
Deployment Pattern
SLA
Monthly Downtime Allowance
Single VM, no AS, Standard HDD
No SLA
Unlimited — no commitment
Single VM, no AS, Premium SSD
~43 minutes/month
2+ VMs in Availability Set
~21.6 minutes/month
2+ VMs across Availability Zones
~4.3 minutes/month
Multi-region active-active
Design-dependent
Approaching 99.999% (5.2 min/year)
Migrating from Availability Sets to Availability Zones

When a region gains Availability Zone support, existing Availability Set deployments do not automatically migrate. You must recreate the VMs in zone-spread configurations. The general migration path is:
Create a snapshot of each OS disk and data disk in the Availability Set
Create managed disks from the snapshots in the target zones
Deploy new VMs in the target zones, attaching the disk copies
Update DNS, load balancer backend pools, and application configuration to point to the new VMs
Validate the new deployment under production load
Decommission the original Availability Set VMs

# Step 1: Create disk snapshot (repeat for each VM's OS and data disks)
az snapshot create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1-os-snapshot \
  --source /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/disks/myVM-1_OsDisk_1

# Step 2: Create a zone-pinned managed disk from the snapshot
az disk create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-1-zone1-os \
  --source myVM-1-os-snapshot \
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --zone 1

# Step 3: Create a new VM in Zone 1 from the disk
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-zone1 \
  --attach-os-disk myVM-1-zone1-os \
  --os-type Linux \
  --zone 1 \
  --size Standard_DS2_v2
Summary

Availability Sets protect VMs within a single datacenter against rack-level hardware failures (fault domains) and ensure that platform updates do not reboot all VMs simultaneously (update domains). They are the appropriate choice when your region lacks Availability Zone support or when you need sub-millisecond latency between co-located VMs.
Fault domains (2-3) spread VMs across separate racks with independent power and network switches.
Update domains (up to 20, default 5) ensure Azure reboots only one domain at a time during planned maintenance.
Always use managed disks (sku: Aligned) so disk fault domains are aligned with VM fault domains.
An Availability Set requires at least 2 VMs to unlock the 99.95% SLA; a single VM gains nothing from the grouping.
Pair Availability Sets with a Standard Load Balancer and health probes to achieve automatic traffic redirection when a VM goes offline.
For regions with Availability Zones, prefer zone-spread deployments (99.99% SLA) over Availability Sets (99.95% SLA) for new workloads.
